planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with fast-draining, gritty, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil — tilted at an angle in a rock crevice or raised bed is ideal — in zone 4–8; Siskiyou Lewisia is a jewel of the Pacific Northwest mountains, native to rocky cliff faces and talus slopes in the Siskiyou Mountains of northern California and southern Oregon — producing beautiful rosettes of succulent, paddle-shaped leaves and stunning sprays of multi-petaled flowers in shades of white, pink, salmon, orange, and magenta in spring.

directions: Plant Lewisia cotyledon in a vertical crevice between rocks, in a trough, or in a raised scree bed with extremely well-drained, gritty soil; the crown must stay dry — plant at a slight angle to shed water; water moderately in spring during active growth, reduce sharply in summer, and avoid any water sitting on the crown which causes rot. Siskiyou Lewisia is one of the most beautiful of all rock garden plants — producing from a thick, carrot-like taproot an evergreen rosette of glossy, succulent leaves and abundant branching sprays of spectacular, 1-inch flowers in brilliant shades of white to deep magenta, often with darker veining or striping, throughout spring. The key to success is excellent drainage: in nature it grows in vertical rock crevices where water cannot stand, and replicates this in the garden are best achieved in rock gardens, dry-stone walls, hypertufa troughs, or steeply tilted raised beds with at least 50% grit or coarse sand. In humid summer climates, provide overhead protection during wet summers to prevent crown rot — a classic cause of failure in the Southeast and Pacific Northwest lowlands. Many cultivars have been selected for exceptionally vivid or bicolored flowers; the 'Sunset Strain' and 'Rainbow Series' offer particularly reliable performance and varied color. Divides readily in early spring from offsets.
sources: North American Rock Garden Society — Lewisia
Oregon State University Extension — Lewisia cotyledon

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 3–4 inches deep in full sun in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, lean, sandy or rocky soil in zone 6–10; keep completely dry from midsummer through fall; Butterfly Mariposa Lily is one of California's most spectacular native bulbs — native to chaparral, oak woodland, and mountain meadows — bearing large, upright, cup-shaped flowers in white to cream, yellow, or deep purple with distinctive butterfly-like markings at the petal bases and a contrasting band of color above the nectary.

directions: Plant Calochortus venustus bulbs in fall in extremely well-drained, lean, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; provide regular water in late winter and spring during active growth, then withhold all water from late spring/early summer through fall to replicate the California dry-summer climate — summer moisture is the primary cause of bulb failure outside California. Butterfly Mariposa Lily is one of the most variable and beautiful of all Calochortus — a large genus of exclusively Western North American native bulbs that has been called 'the lilies that ate California.' The cup-shaped flowers can be white, cream, yellow, deep wine-purple, or lavender, nearly always with bold, dark, butterfly-like blotches at the petal bases in contrasting colors — the effect is like a stained-glass window in miniature. In California gardens with dry summers, they are straightforward bulbs for naturalizing in grassland gardens, among coastal scrub, and on sunny embankments; outside California, success requires excellent drainage and rigorous summer drought. Grow in containers that can be moved under a porch roof in summer, or in raised beds under an overhang. Resist the urge to water in summer — it mimics their native dry-summer climate. Multiplies by offsets and self-sows in suitable conditions.

subcategory: California native dry summer grassland bulb

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 3–4 inches deep in full sun in very well-drained, dry, lean, clay-loam to sandy soil in zone 7–10; withhold water completely from late spring through fall; Yellow Mariposa Lily is a spectacular California native bulb of the Central Valley foothills and inner Coast Ranges — bearing large, bright-golden-yellow, three-petaled, cup-shaped flowers with brown-red markings at the petal bases — one of the easiest Calochortus for drier California gardens and naturalistic plantings.

directions: Plant Calochortus luteus bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in lean, dry, clay-loam or sandy soil in full sun; provide winter and spring moisture (natural rainfall is usually sufficient in California), then allow to go completely dry from June onward — no summer water is essential for long-term success. Yellow Mariposa Lily is one of the most cheerful and showy of all California spring wildflowers — forming grassland and foothill scrub meadows of pure brilliant yellow across the Central Valley foothills when conditions are right. The large, satiny-petaled, chalice-shaped flowers are a bright, clear yellow with a small reddish-brown blotch inside at the base of each petal above the nectar gland. Unlike the more variable Calochortus venustus, the yellow color is consistent and reliable, making it an excellent choice for naturalistic meadow plantings and spring wildflower gardens in California and other dry-summer climates. Calochortus luteus grows naturally in heavy clay-loam soils of California's inner Coast Ranges and foothills — a habitat that floods in winter and bakes concrete-hard in summer — and adapts surprisingly well to similar heavy soils in the garden. An excellent companion to poppies, lupines, and blue dicks in California native wildflower meadows.
sources: California Native Plant Society — Calochortus luteus
Theodore Payne Foundation — growing California native bulbs

subcategory: North Pacific native edible rice-grain bulb

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 4–5 inches deep in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained, peaty or woodland soil in zone 3–8; Black Lily (Kamchatka Fritillary) is a stunning, unusual native bulb of the North Pacific Rim — native from Alaska and coastal British Columbia across the Aleutian Islands to Japan, Korea, and Kamchatka — bearing dramatic, nodding, very dark maroon-brown to near-black flowers with a musky, slightly unpleasant scent that attracts flies as pollinators, and historically used as a food source by Alaska Native peoples.

directions: Plant Fritillaria camschatcensis bulbs in fall 4–5 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, peaty, acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; keep soil consistently moist through the growing season but not waterlogged; mark planting locations as the summer dormancy period makes the site look bare. Black Lily is one of the most dramatically colored of all bulbs — the nodding, tulip-shaped flowers are an exceptionally deep, near-black maroon, often with a slight greenish sheen to the interior, and are held in whorled clusters of 1–8 blooms at the top of the stem. In its natural habitat — the cool, moist, foggy coastlines, alpine meadows, and forest edges of the North Pacific Rim — it grows in rich, peaty, acidic soil in partial shade, and its cultivation requirements mirror these conditions closely. The bulbs consist of a main bulb surrounded by many small rice-grain bulblets (which multiply readily and produce flowering plants in 3–5 years); a distinct slightly unpleasant musty scent of the flowers attracts fly pollinators rather than bees. The bulbs were an important food for many Alaska Native groups including the Tlingit, Haida, and Aleut — boiled or steamed in baskets or pits, the flavor is said to be bitter-starchy but acceptable after preparation. A wonderful and unusual plant for moist, cool, partially shaded Pacific Northwest gardens.
sources: University of Alaska Fairbanks Cooperative Extension — Alaska Native edible plants

subcategory: Pacific Northwest native shade spring bulb

planting.note: Plant corms in fall 3–4 inches deep in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic woodland soil in zone 4–8; White Fawn Lily (Oregon Fawn Lily) is a graceful, spring-blooming native bulb of Pacific Northwest open woodland and forest edges from Vancouver Island and western Oregon to northern California — producing beautiful, reflexed, pure-white flowers with creamy-yellow centers above pairs of handsomely mottled, bronzy-green and silver-grey leaves.

directions: Plant Erythronium oregonum corms in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; keep consistently moist in spring and allow to dry out somewhat in summer during dormancy; plant in colonies of 10–25 for the best naturalistic effect; mark planting areas as the plants disappear completely by midsummer. White Fawn Lily is one of the most elegant of all Pacific Northwest native wildflowers — blooming early in spring before the tree canopy leafs out in the dappled shade of western red cedar, Douglas-fir, and bigleaf maple forests. The reflexed, nodding flowers with their upswept petals (resembling the style of a Turk's Cap lily) are pure white with bright yellow anthers and a cream-yellow center; below the flower, a pair of broad, succulent leaves with beautiful maroon-and-silver mottling emerges from the ground. Naturally forms spreading colonies from corm offsets and self-sowing; in suitable woodland garden conditions it will slowly naturalize and spread to carpet large areas. The mottled leaves are among the most attractive foliage patterns in spring bulbs. An outstanding companion for Trillium, native ferns, Dicentra, and spring wildflowers in Pacific Northwest woodland gardens; tolerates more summer moisture than many Erythronium species.

subcategory: Pacific Northwest native nodding tiger lily

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 4–6 inches deep in full sun to partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 4–9; Columbia Tiger Lily is the iconic native lily of the Pacific Coast from British Columbia to California — producing tall, elegant stems bearing numerous nodding, brilliant-orange, Turk's Cap-style flowers with maroon spots — growing naturally in open woodland margins, meadows, and forest clearings, and historically an important food plant for many Pacific Northwest Indigenous peoples.

directions: Plant Lilium columbianum bulbs 4–6 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; provide consistent moisture through the growing season and avoid waterlogged conditions; mulch with leaf mold to keep roots cool; allow foliage to die back naturally after flowering. Columbia Tiger Lily is one of the most graceful and naturally beautiful of all North American native lilies — a hallmark wildflower of Pacific Northwest meadows, forest openings, and stream-side clearings that blooms in midsummer with dozens of brilliant orange, reflexed flowers spotted with maroon on each tall stem. The lily grows naturally in the transitional zone between open meadow and forest edge, preferring the soil moisture of forest proximity with the light of open areas — conditions replicated in the dappled shade of garden woodland margins. Many Pacific Northwest Indigenous groups — including the Chinook, Quileute, Klallam, and Quinault — ate the bulbs, roasted or boiled, as a significant starchy food source; the bulbs have a pleasantly mild, slightly bitter flavor similar to other edible lilies. An excellent plant for naturalizing in Pacific Northwest native gardens; it self-sows in suitable conditions and multiplies by bulb offsets to form larger colonies over time. Combines beautifully with Camas, Red Columbine, and native ferns.
sources: University of Washington Burke Herbarium — Lilium columbianum
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Lilium columbianum
Native American Ethnobotany Database — Lilium columbianum
